text: BALDERN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Jaxt, 10 m. NE of Neresheim. Pop. 700.
BALDERNOCK, a parish of Stirlingshire, bounded by the Kelvin on the S, which separates it from Lanarkshire. Pop. 972. There are coal-mines and alum-works in this district.
BALDERSLEY, a township in the p. of Topcliffe, in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NNE of Ripon. Area 1,600 acres. Pop. 296.
BALDERSTON, a chapelry in the p. of Blackburn, Lancashire, 6 m. ENE of Preston. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 585.
BALDERTON, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 2 m. SE of Newark. Area 4,050 acres. Pop. 899.—Also a township in Middle p. in Salop. Pop. 26.
BALDICHIERI, a commune and v. of Sardinia, 5 m. NW of Asti.

BALDO (MONTE), a mountain-ridge of Venetian- Lombardy, between the river Adige and the Lago di Garda. It runs close to the shore of the lake, and is connected on the N with the Alps of Tyrol. Its culminating point, the Colma de Sascaga, attains an alt. of 7,120 ft. above sea-level.

BALDOCK, a parish and market-town of Hertfordshire. The town stands in a valley near the source of the Rhea, 37 m. N by W of London. A great quantity of straw-plat is manufactured here. Area of p. 200 acres. Pop. 1,807.
BALDOHN, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Courland, 20 m. E of Mitau, near the river Duna. There are sulphurous springs here.
BALDON-MARSH, a parish in Oxfordshire, 5 m. SE of Oxford. Area 570 acres. Pop. 360.
BALDON-TOOT, a parish adjoining the above. Area 2,010 acres. Pop. 269.
BALDOON, a village of Upper Canada, one of Lord Selkirk's settlements, on the Big Bear river, 10 m. from Lake St. Clair.

BALDOYLE, a p. containing a village of the same name, in co. Dublin. Area 1,236 acres. Pop. in 1831, 1,218; in 1841, 1,100. The village stands on the Portmarnock estuary; and is much frequented in summer as a marine watering-place. Its harbour is nearly dry; admits boats only before last quarterflood; and experiences an average tidal rise of about 12 ft.

BALDRASHANE, or St. Johnstown, a parish partly in co. Antrim, and partly in co. Londonderry. Area 6,361 acres. Pop. in 1831, 2,851; in 1841, 2,658. The surface is part of the low ground along the river Bann, immediately E of the town of Coleraine.
BALDUINSTEIN, a town of Nassau, on the Lahn river. There are marble-works here.
BALDUNGAN, or BALDONGAN, a small parish, 5 m. SE of Balbriggan, in co. Dublin. Area 858 acres. Pop. 118. The surface extends along the sea-coast.

BALDURROCK, an interesting locality, the site BAL of a supposed ancient city, and the scene of a sort of necropolis, on the W coast of the Mullet, about 4 m. SW of Belmullet, co. Mayo. In a great sandy plain along the seaboard, occur a series of small circular areas, stripped, within the last 50 years, of heaps of incumbent sand, and disclosing numerous antique relics of human sepulture.

BALDWIN, a county in the state of Alabama, near the gulf of Mexico. The surface is a sandy plain near the coast, but gradually rises into pineridges in the interior. The chief production is cotton. Area 2,000 sq. m. Pop. 2,951. The cap. is Blakeley.—Also a co. in the central part of and state of Georgia, U. S., watered by the Oconee river. Cap. Milledgeville. Area 216 sq. m. Pop. 2,750.—Also a township in Cumberland co., state of Maine, 40 m. NW of Portland. Pop. 1,150.
BALDWIN. See BRIGHTWELL-BALDWIN.
BALDWINHOLME, a township in the p. of Orton, in Cumberland. Pop. 278.
BALDWINSTOWN, a village in the parish of Garristown, co. Dublin. Pop. 133.—Also a v. in co. Wexford, on the road from Wexford to Clonmines, 2 m. E by N of Duncormuck.
BALDWINSVILLE, a town in Onondago co., in the state of New York, U. S., 145 m. W by N of Albany, on the N side of Seneca river. Pop. 1,000.
BALE. See BASLE.
BALE, or BATHLEY, a parish in the co. of Norfolk, 5 m. W by S of Holt. Area 710 acres. Pop. 229.

BALEA'RIC ISLANDS, the name given by ancient geographers to the group of islands in the Mediterranean lying off the E coast of Spain, stretching in a line nearly 160 m. from NE to SW, and comprehending the islands of Ivica, Majorca, Minorca, Cabrera, Dragonera, Formentara, and upwards of fifty smaller islets. They appear to have been originally peopled by the Phænicians. The Carthaginians, under Hanno, made themselves masters of the group, and a body of their famed slingers accompanied Hannibal in his invasion of Italy. The consul Q. Metellus attached them to the Roman republic as part of Hispania Citerior. The Moors obtained possession of them soon after their invasion of Spain, and were not finally expelled from them till towards the close of the 13th cent. Their total pop. was estimated in 1826 at 250,000.

BALEEK, or Belleek, a quoad sacra parish, containing a v. of the same name, in the quoad sacra p. of Loughgilly, co. Armagh. The village stands 31 m. E of Newtown-Hamilton. Pop. 112. Area of p. about 5,000 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,396.
BALEMAR, an island in the Indian ocean, to the NE of Timor, in S lat. 7° 18'.
BALERMA, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, 3 m. ESE of Mendrisio. Pop. 762.

BALEYA, a country of W Africa; bounded on the N by forests; on the E by Amana; on the S by Sangaran; and on the W by Futa. The inhabitants are tributary to the almamy of the Futa. They are of industrious habits, possess herds of cattle, and manufacture white cloth and earthenware. Their villages usually contain from 100 to 125 huts.—Caillie.
BALFEIGHAN, a parish in co. Meath, about 1 m. N of Kilcock, Leinster. Area 1,617 acres. Pop. in 1841, 152.

BALFRON, a parish and v. of Stirlingshire. Pop. of p. in 1831, 2,057; in 1841, 1,970. The p. is skirted on the S by the Endrick. The v. is 19 m. N of Glasgow, and about the same distance WSW of Stirling. Its pop. in 1841 amounted to 1,568, chiefly composed of handloom weavers, cotton-spinners, and agriculturists.

BALFRUSH, or BALFURUSH, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, on the Bawhol or Bahol, 12 m. above the mouth of that river, which discharges itself into the Caspian, and 27 m. E of Amol, and 30 m. W of Sari. It is the second town of the prov.; and has a pop. of about 25,000; but its site is low and unhealthy, and the roads in the environs are often so flooded as to be impassable. Its principal articles of export are silk and cotton.
BALG, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Murg and Pfinz, 3 m. NNE of Baden. Pop. 419. A fine porcelain clay is procured here.
BALGA, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, 20 m. SW of Königsberg, on the Nauter. Pop. 600.
BALGACH, a village of Switzerland, in the Rheinthal, in the cant. of St. Gall, 12 m. E of St. Gall. Pop. 1,395.
BALGALLEY HEAD, a promontory on the coast of Glenarm, 4 m. N by W of Larne, co. Antrim. The coast here, and northward to Glenarm, is singularly grand and impressive.
BALGONIE, a village in the parish of Markinch, in Fifeshire. Coal and iron-stone are extensively mined in the vicinity.
BALHARI, a town of Hindostan, in the Balaghaut district of Mysore, 187 m. NE of Seringapatam.

BALI, or Bally, an island which lies immediately to the E of Java, from which it is separated by a strait only 3 m. wide at its N entrance. It lies between the parallels of 8° and 9° S lat.; and its S promontory is in S lat. 8° 40', E long. 115° 20'. It is about 70 m. in length from E to W; and has an average breadth of 35 m. A chain of mountains extends along the N coast, running from 5 m. to 10 m. inland, and terminating on the E in the volcanic peak of Bali. Another chain runs in the same direction, about 25 m. to the S of the former. On these mountains are several lakes; and numerous streams descend from them to the coast on all sides. On the lower grounds, rice is extensively cultivated; on the upper, sweet potatoes and maize. Cotton and tobacco are also extensively grown; and cocoa-nuts, oranges, citrons, and other tropical fruits, are abundant; but there are no forests, and timber is consequently scarce. The S side is the best cultivated. Cattle are abundant; and hides form a valuable article of export.—The pop. is estimated at 700,000. The Balinese have hitherto maintained their independence. Their language is rude and peculiar, a compound of Malay and Javanese, with a mixture of Madurese [Medhurst], extending no further, it is alleged, than to the adjoining island of Lambok. Their religion is a modification of Hinduism. The priesthood is hereditary and exclusive. The Balinese abstain from the flesh of the cow, but make no scruple of using buffaloes and pigs for food. They are a fine race of men, and superior in stature and muscular strength to the generality of Eastern islanders. They employ themselves chiefly in the cultivation of the soil; but manufacture iron and steel articles, such as knives, creeses, betel-knippers, and even rifle-barrels. Their dress consists merely of a sarong or chequered cloth round the waist, and falling down to the knees; and a blue and white coarse cloth, thrown over the shoulders, or tied round the middle, and used as a covering at night. They tie heir hair with a strip of cloth or a piece of list. The island is divided into 8 states, which are governed by their own independent rajahs. Of these states, Badong, the extreme southern portion of the island, although one of the smallest, enjoys the most extensive commerce. This state consists of a peninsula of about 50 sq. m., which is joined to the main by a low neck, 2 m. wide, with about the same extent of territory on the main land. During the SE monsoon, ships are perfectly sheltered on the W side of this isthmus; and during the W BAL monsoon, are equally safe on the other side. S winds generally prevail near the S coast of the islands which form this strait from February to September; while from November to March, strong W winds are frequent, producing a westerly current. Vessels should keep mid-channel, as the passage is dangerous, and the tides are strong, and there are numerous eddies which are apt to horse a ship on the rocky shore of Java. [Vogage of Potomac.] The chief produce of Badong, consisting of rice, coffee, and tobacco, is exported to Singapore; the return cargo being European manufactures, opium, and Chinese corn. Intercourse is also maintained by Badong with our Australian colonies and the Mauritius, which receive, by means of British vessels, rice, coffee, Indian corn, cattle, and pigs. The ports next in importance to Badong are Beiling on the N, and Karang-Assam, which are visited by the Chinese, and by Bughi prahus from Singapore. A considerable quantity of coffee is carried to Singapore from the N coast of Bali, the greater portion of which is smuggled from the E districts of Java, where coffee is a governmentmonopoly. [Earl's Report.] No European power has ever made a permanent settlement on this island. Slavery exists in B, as all malefactors become slaves. The Dutch government at Java, and the Chinese, purchase these slaves at about 20 dollars a-head.— Transactions of the London Missionary Society, vol. iii. -Crawford's Indian archipelago. -Singapore Chronicle, 1830.
BALIA, a district in Upper Guinea, 10 journeys E of Timbu, and bounded on the E by the Kankan.
BALIBONICA, a town on the S coast of the island of Cuba, in N lat. 20° 5'.
BALICE, an island off the N coast of Java, near the N entrance of the straits of Sunda, in S lat. 5° 50'.
BALIGNANO, a town of Tuscany, 10 m. N of Grosetto, between the Ombrone and the Buenna.
BALIGROD, a village of Austria, in Galicia, 12 m. SE of Sanok. Pop. 350. In the environs are numerous salt lakes.
BALINGEN. See BAHLINGEN.
BALINTANG, or RICHMOND ISLES, three rocky islets forming the extreme cluster of the Bashi group, in N lat. 19° 57', E long. 122° 16'.
BALIQUATRO ISLES, a group of rocky islets off Baliquatro-point, on the N coast of the isle of Samar, in the Philippine group, in N lat. 12° 40'.
BALIRA, a river of Spain, watering the territory of Andorra, and flowing into the Segre.
BALIS, or Belis, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the r. bank of the Euphrates, in N lat. 36°, 10 m. below Bajik.
BALISAKEERY, or BALLYSAKEERY, a parish in co. Mayo, 25 m. SE of Killalla. Area 12,692 acres. Pop. 6,034. The surface extends along the W bank of the Moy.
BALIYANBOLI, a kadilik of Asiatic Turkey, in Anatolia, in the sanjak of Aidin.
BALIZAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Saint-Symphorien. Pop. 1,038.
BALIZE. See BELIZE.
BALK, a township in the p. of Kirkby-Knowle, N. R. of Yorkshire, 43 m. NE of Thirsk. Area 780 acres. Pop. 89.—Also a town of Holland, in the prov. of Friesland, W of Sloten. Pop. 1,030.
BALKA, a town of European Turkey, in Moldavia, 25 m. W of Birlat, in N lat. 46° 6'.

BALKAN BAY, a shallow gulf on the E coast of the Caspian, in N lat. 40°, into which it is supposed the Oxus, or one of its arms, anciently flowed, between two mountains known as the Great and the Little Balkan. It contains the island of Shileken, the largest island of the Caspian, celebrated for its naphtha springs.

BALKA'N MOUNTAINS, the first great mountain-barrier of Turkey on the north, running between the 42d and 43d parallels, from the termination of the Dinaric Alps, near Perserin, and the sources of the Ibar and Vardar on the W, to the Black sea on the E. The numerous ramifications of this great mountain-system intersect, in various directions, the whole of European Turkey S of the Danube; and divide the basin of the lower portion of that river the sea. from the streams which flow southwards to the archipelago. The main chain presents seven natural divisions. The first, commencing near Perserin, extends from the sources of the Ibar to those of the Sidnitza, under the name of the Shar-dagh or Karadagh, the ancient Mons Scardus. This is a high and extensive chain running SW and NE from the neighbourhood of Alessio or Scutari to a bold conical hill named Luibeten, W of Kacsanik. Seen towards the end of June, from the plains of Albania, it presents a lofty alpine range, with occasional pointed summits, and small fields or patches of snow. Mount Scardus, one of its highest points, may, however, be crossed in a day. The next division bears the names of Gliubolin, Argentaro, and Egrisu or the ancient Mons Orbelus. The eastern part of Egrisu and the mountains of Ghiustendil form the third division. The fourth division of the Balkan terminates to the S of Samakof, in the Dubnitza or Mons Scomius. From Samakof the chain runs E and NE to the sources of the Kamtshik or Panysus. The rivers Vid, Osma, and Jantra, take their rise in this portion of the chain, and flow towards the Danube. From the Kamtshik to the sources of the Tuz-cassri the chain runs E; and from the latter river to Cape Emineh, on the Black sea, it forms a circular sweep. The three latter divisions compose the Emineh-dagh, or ancient Hæmus, which is divided into Veliki Balkan, or the Great Balkan, between Sophia and Keczanlik; and Malo Balkan, or Little Balkan, which forms the E part of this portion of the chain. Dr. Bone is of opinion that the elevation of the Great B. does not greatly exceed 3,000 ft.; and that that of the Little B. scarcely reaches 2,000 ft. Small contreforts, or hills running parallel to each other, exist only on the N side between Shumla and Khabrova; the declivity is more abrupt towards the S. The most elevated summit of the Balkan is the Orbelus, near the sources of the Isker, whose elevation is certainly not under 1,500 toises, or about 9,660 English ft. The principal branches of the Balkan are eight in number, three of which lie on the N side of the main chain. Beginning at the river Ibar, we observe a ridge striking-off at the point of junction of the first and second divisions of the main chain, to the SE of Pristina, and separating the sources of the Eastern Morava from those of the Toplitza: this chain at first bears the name of Gliubolin; but to the E of Novibazar it assumes that of Jaztribovatz. At Mount Kopanneg it divides into two secondary branches, one of which runs W along the Ibar, under the name of Mount Shellian, to its confluence with the Western Morava, and the other terminates in the heights of Jastrebatz, on the l. bank of the Eastern Morava. -At the sources of the Struma or Grand Karasu, a second subsidiary chain strikes-off towards the N, running between the Isker, the Danube, and the Morava. From Ghiustendil to the northern sources of the Isker, and from these to the Nissava, this branch is called the Khoja-Balkan, and runs in a northerly direction. It then runs NW, and takes the denominations of Vidick, Stara, and Herlanie; and turning again N, it receives the name of Haidukki. Near the source of the Boreska, it divides into several branches, the principal one of which runs along the Boreska to the Danube at Trajan's Rock, or the Iron-gate Mountain, opposite Orsova, where the Balkan becomes connected with the Transylvanian or Krapack mountains, the Danube here forcing its way between them. -The third and last northern branch of the Balkan is rather an irregular tract of mountainous country than a series of connected heights. It extends between the tributary streams of the r. bank of the Lower Danube, and those which run directly towards Among the southern ramifications of the Balkan, commencing on the E, we observe a long chain of mountains, which detaches itself at a point about 100 m. from Cape Emineh, and may be regarded as the connecting link betwixt the Balkan and the great chain of Asiatic Turkey, the Taurus. This chain, called the Strandshea, has the Black sea on the NE; the sea of Marmora on the S; and the Tondja and the Erkene, two of the principal tributaries of the Maritza, on the W. It is mostly covered with thick forests; and about 11 leagues from Constantinople it presents a series of fertile and cultivated eminences. Near the sources of the Erkene, a secondary branch strikes-off from the Strandshea, towards the SW, and joins the Tekinr-dagh, the prolongment of which forms the peninsula of Gallipoli. - Proceeding westwards we find the Despoti-dagh,-the Rhodope of the ancients, -striking-off from the main chain at Dubnitza, and running SE towards the sources of the Arda, round which it takes a semicircular sweep, and then runs nearly E to Cape Makri. This is a very lofty and rugged chain, diminishing in height as it proceeds E. The W part is called Rilo-planina, and Rilo-dagh; further to the E, it takes the names of its great valleys. The name Despoti-dagh, [i. e. 'Chain of the Ecclesiastics,'] originated with the Greek convents situated on this chain. The Perin-dagh, which lies between Djuma, Mehinck, and Nerrokup, is a ramification of the Despoti-dagh. The highest summits in these two chains probably do not exceed 7,000 ft. They present a bold appearance as seen from the N side; but diminish in height as they approach the sea on the S. They are well-wooded with oak, larch, and fir, in the central and higher parts; with Alpine pastures and bare summits above.—The Karasu or Nev-ro-kop-dagh strikes-off from the main chain with the Despoti-dagh, and terminates near Cape Asperosa, opposite to the island of Thassos.—Between the Struma on the E, and the Egridere on the W, runs a long ridge of mountains bearing the name of Cercine or Kerkine: extending along the Vardar to its embouchure, this ridge divides itself into three branches, which running into the sea form the gulfs of Salonica and Contessa. The passes of the Balkan are nearly impracticable during winter. In the first division of the main chain the pass of Katshianik, by the Tshar-dagh, establishes a communication between Servia and Romelia, or the towns of Pristina and Uskub. It is defended by fortified works. In the second subdivision of the Balkan there are five passes. One of these passes is under the Gliubolin-dagh, and another under Mount Argentaro; the other three are narrow cols communicating with the two former.-Across the third section of the Balkan, two foot-roads lead from Ghiustendil to Medolsa and Shardak.-In the fourth division a foot-road conducts from Sophia to Dubnitza, and three foot-roads from Sophia to Ghiustendil, and from Samakof to Dubnitza and Menlik .- In the fifth subdivision there are six passes within a line of 51 leagues. Three of these lead from Samakof to Bagna and Kostendje; a fourth extends from Ikliman on the Vid, to Tatar-Bazardjik on the Maritza; the fifth communicates with Loftcha in Bulgaria; and the sixth extends between the towns of Kabrova and Kezanlik, forming a part of the great road from Rust- 別な話した初 chuk to Adrianople.—The pass of Demir-Kapi, communicating betwixt Stareka in Bulgaria on the Kamtshik, and Selimno on the Islandjik, is the only practicable pass in the sixth section.—In the last subdivision there are three defiles, none of which are formidable even to the passage of cannon. Two of these begin at Carnabat in Romelia, and proceeding northwards, communicate, one on the W with Eski- Juma, and the other on the E with Shumla; the third pass opens a communication betwixt Varna and Burgas, both on the coasts of the Black sea.—Of all these passes the most remarkable is the Sulu-Derbend or Porta Trajani, through which the great road from Vienna to Constantinople, by Belgrade and Sophia, runs. It is a deep defile defended by two forts, the one at Ikliman, and the other near Kostenje. BALKASH, BHALKHASHI-NOR, OF PALKATI, a large lake of Turkistan, on the Chinese frontier, between N lat. 42° and 45°, and at an elevation of 800, or perhaps 1,000 ft. above sea-level. It is said to be 15 days' journey in circumf., and to exceed 100 m. in length; but its breadth must be considerably less. The steppes of the Khirghiz Kasaks extend from its N extremity to the lake of Aral. The river Ili flows into it on the S, and it receives several other large streams, but has no visible outlet.

BALKH, or BAULKH, a large region of Bokhara, to the SE of the Turkoman deserts, corresponding to the western and larger division of the ancient Bactria. It extends from 63° to 70° E long., and from 35° and 36° to 37° and 38° N lat. It is impossible, from want of proper data, to arrive at precision in describing its extent and boundaries; but it may, in a general way, be estimated at 400 m. from E to W, by 150 m. of general breadth: thus comprising a surface of 60,000 sq. m. It has Badakshan on the E; the Oxus on the N; the desert of Margiana on the NW; the district of Herat on the SW; and on the S the Paropamisan mountains, with the western projection of the Hindukush. It is a highly diversified region of mountains, hills, glens, valleys, and plains. The subalpine ridges, below the Paropamisus and the Hindukush, occupy half its surface; and several of the lateral ridges in the eastern division approach the Oxus. To the W, the country expands into wide and continuous plains, increasing in similarity to the deserts of the Turkoman tribes, as they proceed in that direction, till they become completely identified with them. The whole district was subject in 1825 to Mir Morad Khan, son of Khaldad Khan of Kunduz, who, on the death of his uncle, Mir Killich Ali, chief of Khulum, seized on his territory and that of Balkh, and made himself master of the whole country southwards to the pass of Bamian and the border of the Hazarehs. The Usbeks are the predominating people in all this region, and are divided into many tribes. They are all rigid Sunnis, and are much influenced by their mollahs, who have a predominant sway in all their councils, and exercise a kind of theocratical power over them. The river of Balkh, which gives name to the prov. and its cap., rises in the Hindukush, and enters the great Turkistan plain about 6 m. S of Balkh, whence it flows to the Oxus.—The city of Balkh is situated in a large and fertile plain, 25 m. S of the Oxus, at an alt. of about 1,800 ft. above sea-level; in E long. 67° 18', N lat. 36° 48'. It was once a magnificent and extensive city; but ravaged successively by Jenghiz Khan, by Tamerlane, and by Nadir Shah, it is now little more than a mass of ruins, amongst which some 2,000 or 3,000 Usbeks and Afghans have located themselves. It was the Zariaspa or Bactra of the Greeks.—Conolly.—Burnes.
BALKHOLME, a township in the p. of Howden, E. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. E of Howden. Area 550 acres. Pop. 165. BAL
BALKIS, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in N lat. 40° 23', near Cape Melanos, and on the ruins of the ancient Cyzicus.

BALL, or BALLAGH, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in co. Mayo, Connaught. The village is 73 m. ESE of Castlebar. Though much spoken of by ancient chroniclers, it is now a small collection of poor houses.
BALLA (MOUNT), a mountain in Sudan, in N lat. 8° 43', 120 m. S of Mora.

BALLACHULISH, a district lying along the Linnhe-loch. It is chiefly in the p. of Kilmalie, in Inverness-shire, but extends into Argyleshire. Pop. of the whole district in 1841, 1,235, of whom 279 were in the village of B., and chiefly employed in extensive slate quarries in the neighbourhood.
BALLAGH, or Belough, a village in the parish of Clonoulty, co. Tipperary. Pop. 217.

BALLAGH, or Funcheon, a lake nearly in the centre of the barony of Athlone, co. Roscommon. Its length is about 2 m. Two small streams enter it on the NW; but all surplus waters are supposed to pass through swallow holes into subterranean channels.
BALLAGHADIREEN, a post town in the parish of Kilcoleman, co. Mayo. It stands on the road from Longford to Ballina, 14 m. NW of Tulsk, and 14 m. ESE of Swineford.
BALLAGHKEEN, a hamlet in the parish of Ballyhuskard, co. Wexford. Pop. 132.
BALLAGHMOON, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. SW by S of Castle-Dermot. Area 2,178 acres. Pop. 308.
BALLAIGNES, a village of Switzerland, in the canton of Vaud, and district of Orbe. Pop. 500.
BALLAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Montbazon. Pop. 1,192.
BALLANE, or BULLANE, a parish 45 m. N of the town of Loughrea, co. Galway. Area 1,729 acres. Pop. 445.

BALLANO, or CHEPO, a river of Central America, which rises in the prov. of Darien, runs W, and in some degree parallel to the coast, and then suddenly turns S, and enters the Pacific, in the gulf of San Miguel, a few miles E of the Pacora.

BALLANTRAE, a parish and v. in the SE of Ayrshire, extending 12 m. along Loch-Ryan and the coast of the Irish sea. Pop. in 1,801, 837; in 1841, 1,651, of whom 256 were in the v. of B., which is 17 m. from Stranraer, and 12 m. from Girvan. A fishing-harbour has recently been formed here.
BALLAPUR, a town of Hindestan, in the prov. of Berar, 15 m. W of Akolah.
BALLARD, a headland in co. Clare, on the S side of the entrance of Dunbeg bay, 143 m. NE of Loop-head lighthouse.
BALLARD, a county in the state of Kentucky, U. S., bounded by the Mississippi on the W, and the Ohio on the N. Area 375 sq. m. Capital, Columbus.
BALLASORE. See BALASORE.

BALLATER, a village in the p. of Glenmuick, in Aberdeenshire, 41 m. W of Aberdeen, on the river Dee. At Pannanich, 13 m. below B., are chalybeate wells, which attract numerous visitors to B. during the summer-months.
BALLAUGH, a parish and v. in the isle of Man, 7 m. SW of Ramsay. Pop. 1,516.
BALLEE, or Bally, a parish 2 m. SE of Downpatrick, co. Down. Area 6,428 acres. Pop 2,255.

BALLEEN, a parish 2 m. SW of Freshford, in co. Kilkenny. Area 2,559 acres. Pop. 837. The highest ground has an alt. of 635 ft. The castle of B., an imposing ruin, here crowns a slowly ascending yet rather lofty hill. It was a principal stronghold of the earls of Ormonde.
BALLENBERG, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, 7 m. SW of Boxberg. Pop. 540.
BALLENSTEDT, a town and bailiwick of Anhalt-Bernburg, 15 m. SE of Halberstadt, at the foot of the Harz mountains, and watered by the Getel river. Pop. 3,800.

BALLENY ISLES, a group of five islands, 3 large, and 2 small, in the Antarctic ocean, of which the central island is in S lat. 66° 44', E long. 163° 11', discovered by Captain Balleny, on the 9th and 10th of Feb. 1839. The highest, named Young island, rises in a beautiful peak to an alt. of 12,000 ft.

BALLERNY, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Bayeux.—The cant. comprises 29 com., and had a pop. of 16,080 in 1831.—The com., which is 10 m. SW of Bayeux, has a pop. of 1,300. Silk lace is made here to a considerable extent.
BALLERSDORF, or BADRICOURT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Altkirch. Pop. 717.
BALLESTERO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Albacete, in the partido of Alcaraz. Pop. 1,180. It has a type-foundry and a tobacco manufactory.
BALLICKMOYLER, a village in the p. of Killeban, Queen's co. Area 12 acres. Pop. 294. Houses 48. It stands about 5 m. S by W of Athy.

BALLINA, a post, market, and seaport town, in the parish of Kilmoremoy, co. Mayo. It stands on the l. bank of the Moy, 5 m. S of the point where that stream begins to expand into Killalla bay, and 9 m. N of Fexford. Excepting Ballinasloe and Westport, no town in Connaught excels B. in cleanliness, neatness and regularity. B., in the large and popular sense, includes both B. proper and Ardnaree; and thus understood, is the third in bulk and commercial importance of the towns of Connaught, being inferior only to Galway and Sligo. Two bridges connect the two parts of the town; above these are considerable rapids and a salmon-weir, where salmon are caught in such numbers that an annual rental of £1,000 is said to be paid for the fishery. A courthouse is the most noticeable civil public building. From the excellent fishing the Moy affords, and its proximity to Lough Conn, B. is the resort of many anglers during the summer season. It is also the principal road to the wild district of Erris. The Moy is navigable to within a mile of the town, and brings the tide up quite to the bridges. During a series of years preceding 1833, the exports, consisting chiefly of grain, amounted annually to about 10,000 tons. In 1835, the exports were valued at £70,568. -In 1798, the French, under the command of General Humbert, were, for a brief period, in possession of B. They arrived in Killalla bay, in 3 frigates from Rochelle, on the 22d of August, and consisted of 1,100 men. See Castlebar. Area of the town, exclusive of Ardnaree, 121 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,510; in 1841, 5,313.—Also a village in the p. of Templekelly, co. Tipperary, on the left bank of the Shannon. It is connected with the town of Killaloe by a bridge of 19 arches. Pop. in 1841, 774.
BALLINABOY, a parish in co. Cork. Length 3½ m.; breadth, 2¾ m.; Pop. 2,749. Houses 460. The surface is drained eastward by the Annabuoy.
BALLINACALLY, a village in the p. of Kilchrist, co. Clare, near the estuary of the Fergus, 61 m. SSW of Clare. Pop. 194. Houses 29.
BALLINACARGY, a village in the p. of Kilbixy, co. Westmeath, 5 m. E by S of Colehill. Pop. in 1841, 483.—Also a v. in the p. of Drong, co. Cavan, 6 m. SW by W of Cootehill. Pop. 85.
BALLINACARRIG, a parish in co. Carlow, 14 m. SSE of Carlow. Area 2,605 acres. Pop. 692.— BAL Also a hamlet, the site of a curious old castle, in co. Cork, about 8 m. W of Bandon.
BALLINACARRIGA, a village in the p. of Kilnamanagh, co. Cork. Pop. 152.
BALLINACLASH, a quoad sacra parish in the civil p. of Rathdrum, co. Wicklow. Area about 30,000 acres. The v. of B. stands about 1 m. SW of Rathdrum.
BALLINACLOUGH, a parish in co. Tipperary. Area 3,869 acres. Pop. 1,152.—Also a parish in co. Limerick. Area 1,092 acres. Pop. 630. It lies about 8 m. NW of Tipperary.
BALLINACOR, one of four denominations which constitute the ecclesiastical p. of Rathdrum, in co. Wicklow. Area 17,449 acres. Pop. 1,359.

BALLINACOURTY, a village, and a low headland, at the E side of the entrance of Dungarvan harbour, in co. Waterford. The headland is called both Ballinacourty-point, and Wyse's point. Vessels of large burden may safely run behind it at low water, and enjoy good shelter. The village stands near the extremity of the headland, about 2 m. E by S of Abbeyside.—Also a maritime parish at the head of the bay of Galway, in co. Galway, Connaught. Area 6,293 acres. Pop. 3,407.—Also a parish in co. Kerry, part of the roughly tumulated peninsula along the N coast of Dingle bay, 8 m. NE by E of Dingle. It contains the hamlets of Annascalle and Ballinclare. Area 5,318 acres. Pop. 1,472.
BALLINACURRA, or BALLINACORRA, a village in the p. of Middleton, co. Cork, on the Middleton river, a mile below the town of Middleton.
BALLINADEE, a parish, containing a v. of the same name, in co. Cork. Area 8,334 acres. Pop. 3,038. The v. is 3 m. E of Banden. Pop. 284.
BALLINADRIMNA, a parish in co. Kildare. Area 4,285 acres. Pop. 1,501.
BALLINAFAD, a village in the p. of Aughanah, co. Sligo, 4 m. NNW of Boyle. Pop. 155.
BALLINAFAGH, or BALLYNEFAGH, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. WNW of Prosperous. Area 4,155 acres. Pop. 929.
BALLINAFAUNA, a village in the p. of Clondulane, co. Cork. Pop. 318.
BALLINAGALL, a fishing village and a creck on the S side of Dungarvan harbour, 3 m. S of the town of Dungarvan, co. Waterford.
BALLINAGAR, a village in the p. of Geashil, King's co., 2 m. SW of Philipstown. Pop. 107.
BALLINAGERAH, a village in the p. of Kilcarragh, co. Kerry. Pop. 139. BALLINAGH, BALLINANAGH, or BELLANANAGH, a small town in the p. of Kilmore, co. Cavan, 44 m. SSW of Cavan. Pop. 836.
BALLINAGORE, a village in the p. of Newtown, co. Westmeath. Pop. 125.
BALLINAGORY, or BALLYMAGORRY, a village in the p. of Leckpatrick, co. Tyrone, 2 m. N of Strabane. Pop. 213.

BALLINAHAGLISH, a parish in co. Mayo. 23 m. S by E of Ballina. Area 12,669 acres. Pop. 5,397. —Also a parish 5 m. W of Tralee, co. Kerry. It contains the villages of Chapeltown and Kilfinura. Area 3,006 acres. Pop. 2,147.
BALLINAHASSIG, or BALLINASSIG, a village in the p. of Ballinaboy, co. Cork, 6 m. S of Cork, and about the same distance N of Kinsale. Pop. 152.

BALLINAHINCH, a lake, a rivulet, and a village, in co. Galway. The lake is the most easterly of the chain of romantic lakes which extends through Cunnemara, and whose other chief links are Loughs Derryclare, Ina, and Garromin. It washes the southern slopes of the Lottery mountain,-the extreme or front height of the Binnabola group of Alps, and it sends off to the sea its superfluent waters under the name of the B. river. This stream expands into a long sinuous estuary at the head of Birterbuy bay, and possesses the fame of being the best salmon-fishing stream in Connaught.—Also a small town in the parish of Magheradroll, co. Down, on the Annacloy or B. river, 4 m. SW of Saintfield. About 2 m. SSW of the town, on the skirts of Slieve- Croob, is a chalybeo-sulphureous spa. Pop. 911.

BALLINAKILL, a market and post-town, and formerly a parliamentary borough, in the p. of Dysert-Gallen, Queen's co. It stands in the midst of a fertile plain, 2 m. E of the river Nore, 3 m. SE of Abbeyleix, and 524 m. SW by S of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 1,927; in 1841, 1,540.—Also a large Highland parish on the coast of co. Galway. Area 49,052 acres. Pop. in 1831, 7,183; in 1841, 7,928. The four principal mountains have an alt. above sea-level of respectively 1,172, 1,536, 1,973, and 2,193 ft.; the last of these, Letterbreckan, is situated in the SE corner. A few minor islands off the coast are included within the limits. A spot on one of these inlets is the site of a small pier. At the head of B. bay, there is a flat of about 1,000 acres, on which some attempts have been made at drainage.—Also a parish 64 m. W by N of Roscommon, partly in co. Roscommon, but chiefly in co. Galway. Area of the Roscommon section, 2,006 acres; of the Galway section 12,571 acres. Pop. of the Roscommon section, 565; of the Galway section, 4,433.—Also a parish in co. Galway, 6 m. WSW of Portumna. It contains the village of Woodford. Area 59,606 acres. Lough Derg expands along the E; and the Slieve Baughta mountains occupy a large aggregate area in the W and S. A height near the centre, another in the W, and the Scalp mountain in the SW, have altitudes of respectively 562, 692, and 1,074 ft.— Also a parish in co. Sligo, 5 m. SW of Dromahaire. Area 4,590 acres. Pop. 1,906.—Also a parish in King's co., 3 m. S of Edenderry. Area 6,761 acres. Pop. 1,079.—Also a parish in co. Waterford. Area 1,877 acres. Pop. 639. Little Island, in the Suir, belongs to the parish; it is about a mile in length, and 180 acres in area.
BALLINAKILTY, a parish 12 m. N by E of Mount Bellew, in co Galway. Area 5,221 acres. Pop. 1,762.
BALLINALACK, a village in the p. of Leney, co. Westmeath, on the river Inny, 1 m. above Lough Iron. Pop. 312.
BALLINALEE, a village in the p. of Clonbroney, 6 m. NNW of Edgeworthstown, co. Longford. Pop. 299.
BALLINAMAGHARY, a hamlet in the p. of Carlingford, co. Louth. Pop. 94.
BALLINAMALLARD, or BELLANAMALLARD, a village in the p. of Magheracross, co. Fermanagh, 3½ m. E of Lower Lough Erne.
BALLINAMARA, or Ballynemara, a parish 31/4 m. S of Freshford, co. Kilkenny. Area 3,839 acres. Pop. 915.
BALLINAMEEN, a village in the p. of Desertoghill, co. Londonderry. Pop. 192.
BALLINAMONA, a village in the p. of Dunkitt, co. Kilkenny. Pop. 125.—Also a parish 5 m. ENE of Bruff, co. Limerick. Area, 1,498 acres. Pop. 587.
BALLINAMORE, a post and market-town in the parish of Outragh, co. Leitrim, 12 m. NW of Carrick-on-Shannon, and 71 m. NW of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 312; in 1841, 946.

BALLINAMUCK, a village in the p. of Killoe, co. Longford, 81 m. NNE of Longford. Pop. 163. The v. gives name to the last action of the French army which invaded Ireland at Killalla in 1798. General Humbert, after the various fortunes of his brief campaign, found himself in such a desperate ##1#1# BAL position at B. that he surrendered. His troops were found to consist of 748 privates and 96 officers; so that his entire loss from the time of landing at Killalla to the 8th of Sept., was 256.
BALLINAMUDDAGH, a village in the p. of Ballyhuskard, co. Wexford. Pop. 205.
BALLINARD, a parish 3 m. E by S of Six-mile- Bridge, co. Limerick. Area 1,442 acres. Pop. 918.
BALLINAS, or Balliness, a rivulet of co. Donegal. It traverses an upland region, and enters the sea at a point about 6 m. SW of Horn-head. It is tidal, and navigable over 2 m. above its mouth.
BALLINASCREEN, a parish 23 m. W of Tubbermore, in co. Londonderry. It contains the village of Draperstown. Area 32,492 acres. Pop. 8,384.

BALLINASKELLIGS, a bay on the coast of co. Kerry, between Hog-head on the E and Bolus-head on the W; about 5 m. broad between these promontories, and sweeping inland to the extent of about 6 m. The Hog islands, and several other islets, lie from 23 to 43 m. S of Hog-head, and serve to break the swell of the Atlantic. Horse island lies about 3 m. inward from each of the headlands. The Skelligs rocks lie about 7 m. to seaward, in N lat. 51° 46' 10", W long. 10° 34' 10". On the Great Skellig,— which is a lofty rock of slate rising several hundred ft. above the level of the sea,—two lighthouses have been erected, bearing from each other N by E 650 ft., and at the respective heights of 372 ft. and 173 ft. above high water. There is a harbour on the W side of the bay, contiguous to Ballinaskelligs island, and about 44 m. from Bolus-head; provided with a pier 172 ft. in length. At the head of the bay stands the village of Waterville, and on a creek projecting from it, the celebrated Derrynane abbey.
BALLINASLANEY, a parish in co. Wexford, 43 m. S by E of Enniscorthy. Area 2,646 acres. Pop. 1,061.

BALLINASLOE, a market and post-town, partly in the p. of Creagh, co. Roscommon, but chiefly in the p. of Kilchooney, co. Galway. It stands on both banks of the river Suck, 9 m. NNW of Eyre-court, 121 m. SW of Athlone. The passage across the river at the town is a series of bridges and causeways, carried from island to island, and from stream to stream, upwards of 500 yards. The Roscommon section of the town has the appearance of an extended village. At its extremity stands the district lunatic asylum for the 5 counties of Connaught. The Galway section of the town is very much the larger and more beautiful, and exclusively the seat of business. The principal streets are wide, airy, and clean. The remarkably handsome and unique church, with its singularly beautiful and octangular spire, springing from scrolls, and shooting far aloft from the crown of the gravel ridge, is a striking object itself, and forms a remarkable feature in the landscape for many miles round. The military barrack for infantry is a small and unattractive mass of masonry. B. is both a stirring thoroughfare, and the scene of a large aggregate of traffic. During 10 years, preceding 1836, the quantity of grain sold in the market was 6,435 tons of wheat, 2,756 of barley, and 21,226 of oats; in the last of these years, it was 780 tons of wheat, 48 of barley, and 2,502 of oats. For a series of years the quantity of wool brought to the market chiefly at a great wool fair held in the month of July-was from 560 to 720 tons; yet this is said to have been exceeded fourfold or fivefold by the quantity sold under the market's influence, but without being brought to the town for sale. A fair well-known to fame as "the great fair of Ballinasloe," is held from the 5th till the 9th of October. The average number of sheep and black cattle brought in for sale, is computed to be respectively 90,000 and 12,000; and the greatest number of sheep sold in [ any year between 1790 and 1840, was 86,374, in the year 1828; and of black cattle, was 11,163, in the year 1840. Area of the Roscommon section of the town, 9 acres; of the Galway section, 131 acres. Pop. of the whole, in 1831, 4,615; in 1841, 4,934. Houses 619.
BALLINAULTIG, a parish in co. Cork, Munster. Area 4,842 acres. Pop. 1,404.
BALLINCALLA, or Ballinchola, a parish, partly in co. Galway, partly in co. Mayo, 4 m. S of Ballinrobe. Area 15,195 acres. Pop. 2,165.

BALLINCOLLIG, a post-town in the p. of Carrigrohane, co. Cork. It stands on the river Bride, about a mile above that stream's confluence with the Lee, 5 m. WSW of Cork. An extensive gunpowder manufactory exists here. Pop. 1,287.
BALLINCREA, a village in the p. of Kilcollumb, co. Kilkenny. Pop. 197.
BALLINCUSLANE, or BALLYCUSLANE, a parish in co. Kerry, 92 m. NW of Mill-street, Ulster. Area 39,740 acres. Pop. 5,701.
BALLINDANGAN, or BALLINDINE, a hamlet in the p. of Crossboyne, co. Mayo, 31 m. SE of Claremorris. Pop. 448.

BALLINDERRY, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in co. Antrim. Area 10,891 acres. Pop. 5,679.—Also a parish, partly in co. Tyrone, partly in co. Londonderry, 4 m. SE of Moneymore. Area 8,178 acres. Pop. 3,362.
BALLINDOON, a parish in co. Galway, 7 m. S by E of Clifden. Area 20,033 acres. Pop. 5,615. It forms part of the sea-board of Cunnemara.
BALLINDREAT, a village in the p. of Lifford or Clonleigh, co. Donegal, on the Dale rivalet, 2 m. WNW of Lifford. Pop. 174.
BALLINEA, a village in the p. of Mullingar, co. Westmeath. Pop. 109.—Also a v. in the p. of Nobber, co. Meath. Pop. 147.
BALLINEDDAN, a fishing-village on the N side of Carlingford bay, 3 m. S of Rosstrevor, co. Down.
BALLINEEN, a village in co. Cork, on the l. bank of the Bandon, about 5 m. E by N of Dunmanway. Pop. 670.
BALLINEETY, a village in the p. of Cahirnurry, co. Limerick. Pop. 231.
BALLINGADDY, a parish in co. Limerick, 24 m. E by S of Kilmallock. Area 5,999 acres. Pop. 1,761.

BALLINGARRY, a parish, containing a town of the same name, in co. Limerick. Area 17,732 acres. Pop. 8,679. The town is 4 m. from Rathkeale. Pop. 1,690.—Also a parish in co. Limerick. Area 6,114 acres. Pop. 2,834.—Also a parish 3½ m. ENE of Borris-o-kane, in co. Tipperary. Area 6,683 acres. Pop. 1,962. Lough Nahinch, on the S border, has an elevation of 248 ft.
BALLINGARRY, or GARE, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in co. Tipperary. Area 13,714 acres. Pop. 7,062. The v. of B. stands 5 m. NW of Callan. Pop. 643.
BALLINGDON, a chapelry in Essex, 1 m. SW of Sudbury. Area 730 acres. Pop. 843.
BALLINGHAM, a parish in Hereford, 8 m. SE of Hereford. Area 950 acres. Pop. 149.

BALLINGRAY, a parish in Fifeshire, to the S of Portmoak. It is about 3\frac{1}{2} m. in length, by 1\frac{1}{2} m. in breadth. Pop. in 1801, 277; in 1841, 436. Coal is extensively wrought in this p. BALLINLANDERS, BALLINLONDRY, OF BALLYLANDERS, a parish containing a v. of the same name, in co. Limerick. Area 7,717 acres. Pop. 4,000. The v. is 94 m. ESE of Kilmallock. Pop. 340. BALLINLOGHY, BALLINLOUGH, or BALLYLOUGH, a parish in co. Limerick, 51 m. ESE of Sixmile-Bridge. Area 2,340 acres. Pop. 1,158. - I. BAL
BALLINLOUGH, a village in the p. of Kilskyre, co. Meath. Pop. 117.—Also a village in the p. of Kiltullagh, co. Roscommon, 43 m. W of Castlerea.
